translation.content: When mulling which direction to go in for RA's 1000th mix celebrations, many options came to mind. Some impossible-level IDM icon? A certain semi-anonymous producer 2-stepping around the margins of our collective consciousness? All tempting. But, ultimately, we are a DJ-forward publication and this is a DJ mix series. It felt truer to the history of the RA Podcast to release deep vault material from a time when the world of niche records felt different, tighter, more discrete. The fourth-longest mix in the series' history is an unrepeatable marathon set recorded in 2012 at a superclub that no longer exists. (2012, incidentally, is farther away from 2025 than 2012 was from 2000; if we have to clock it, so do you.) It's the unison of one British icon who passed away in 2020, and another whose time on the road has scaled back considerably as of late. DJ Harvey agreed to exactly one b2b set in his life: this one, with Andrew Weatherall. Although a serial collaborator in the studio, he didn't actually play too many b2b sets either, preferring to sail the open seas by his own navigation. What follows is 385 scintillating minutes of arpeggiated chug and slow-cresting climaxes, all of which makes some of the more headstrong RA.1000s (Sama', Tim Reaper, Jyoty) seem as if they've been beamed in from another artistic medium entirely. The night took place at Trouw, the Amsterdam club whose immaculate decor and labyrinthian immersion was legendary long before it shut up shop in the opening hours of 2015. As part of a series anchored around start-to-close combinations, RA worked with founder Olaf Boswijk and finally put these two peers together in the booth. Weatherall, with infinite brownie points stockpiled from the '90s, remained everyone's favourite debonair psychonaut. Harvey was at the peak of an irresistible career second act, which dovetailed with a disco revival that dominated clubs for years—a moment when the resting heart rate of dance floors plunged lower than potentially any comparable point in the 21st century. If you've got time to spare, a fun side game is sussing out who's playing what. Take the goosebumps-inducing slide into a disco-dub cover of Echo & the Bunnymen's "The Killing Moon"—smart money's on Weatherall. Exuberant EQ'ing of the comically overripe bass on The Isley Brothers' "Live It Up, Pts. 1 & 2"? Gotta be Harvey. As for the low 'n slow, lightly spangled house that was all the rage in the early 2010s (think Maxxi Soundsystem, Disco Bloodbath, Rub & Tug, C.O.M.B.I. and Full Pupp), it's anyone's guess. The pair putter around the 100 BPM range for so long that nudging up to 127 by the double encore feels practically like flooring it down the highway. Long story short, it's a masterclass. When we kicked off RA.1000, we outlined a few goals: convince a handful of dream guests, honour architects who shaped the music around us and deliver recordings you can't find anywhere else. Full send. We equally wanted to render an accurate picture of DJ culture in 2025, futureproof our archives and get arms around some of the key storylines in the decade since we went 5 for RA.500 ten years ago. Who broke big? What could you pull off at peak time today that you'd struggle to make land then? And what memories do we hold more fondly as time has gone on? DJing and the mythmaking around it has undergone a quantum leap since 2012, let alone 2006, 1996 or 1989. It's a scarcely-recognisable scene. For those of us who were kicking around in the former, there's a creeping melancholy that our prime is fast becoming a matter of historical record. The killing moon really did come too soon. There's a sense of fulfilment bound up within that melancholy, though. Appreciation, too. 1000 episodes is great innings, and we're thankful for every contributor and facilitator who built this series, week by week, mix by mix. Where will electronic music, DJ sets or any of this be in 2044? Hard to say; best not to overthink that one. Instead, luxuriate in the company of two of the greatest to ever do it, together, for the first and last time.⁠ – Gabriel Szatan As far as we can tell, this is the first and only time you played together. How do you remember the chemistry playing out in the booth? DJ Harvey: Andy and I had a mutual respect for one another as DJs and so I was not adverse to playing b2b with him, though I generally consider that form of DJing is, oftentimes, a gimmick. As far as the chemistry is concerned, there was no pre-planning, we decided to just vibe off each other. It was a fun night and really a celebration of Trouw as a leading force in Amsterdam clubbing at the time. I decided to keep that as my only ever b2b to honour Andy, Olaf, the club and the night itself. I also don't do or share too many mixes—I don't want to add to the noise. In recent years they've been for good causes only, so I was, of course, happy to give permission for this one to be out there when Andy's family and you all approached us. You and Andrew had overlapped for many years throughout the '90s. He was a big fan of your project Locussolus's "Next to You," and turned in a particularly wonky remix of "Gunship," too. Ah, that infamous Gunship mix. Heidi [Lawden] sent him a mic test vocal I'd done really late one night that wasn't meant to be heard; she knew Andrew and I shared a certain dark sense of humour and he'd appreciate it. Of course he did, and used it for his remix. This recording not only canonises your pairing, but Trouw, which was one of the world's best clubs at the time. What are your memories of the space? I always really enjoyed playing at Trouw and Olaf has a great bedside manner. I was always treated really good and well received by the crowd. The space was quite strange because it was an ex newspaper printing factory, and although it was a bizarre shape, it had been acoustically treated to accommodate industrial machines in a residential area, so the acoustics were surprisingly good. If you have any additional memories about Andrew, we'd love to hear them. I only recently worked out that Andy and I are exactly the same age, and so we had very similar cultural upbringing and references, being southern Englishmen. I would always gravitate towards Andy's sets at parties like Shoom and Heidi's Yellow Book Club, because of his punk rock brand of psychopop, as I called it at the time. We always got along really well and spent most of the time taking the direct piss out of anyone that remotely deserved it. He may have come across as quite serious, but we spent most of our time laughing together. We had a lot of musical reference points that just didn't factor on other people's radars. Andy and me, we came from a long history of collecting records, being in bands. I think we truly weren't following anyone but our own compasses. Statement on behalf of Andrew Weatherall's family Lizzie Walker (partner): We are delighted that this is seeing the light of day. The love, respect and affection that Andrew continues to receive is heartwarming and strengthening to us as a family. We hope that his ethos continues to inspire creativity in this generation and generations to come. Thank you, Lizzie and Ian
translation.blurb: For RA.1000, the rarest of sets: Andrew Weatherall's first official posthumous mix, and the only DJ Harvey b2b in history. Recorded at Trouw in 2012, this one's special.
